what_it_does: Defines the permission resource contract for Claude Code sessions: the tool-approval posture (a closed mode plus allow/deny/ask rule lists), documents the current fabric-wide read-only posture, and specifies the read op permission.get and the mostly planned setter op permission.act.
observations: Front matter marks it as a contract-entry of type permission with status building and empty schemes.
The posture is identified by the session it governs; there is no standalone permission:// addressable record.
Today the only live lever is the fabric-wide posture read via GET /health .permission; per-session mode and rules are the planned extension.
The mode enum is default/acceptEdits/plan/dontAsk/bypassPermissions/auto, with auto noted as TypeScript-SDK only.
Every spawned session currently gets exactly one fixed allow rule, mcp__company; native tools fall through to the mode.
permission.get is explicitly described as the same wire read as fabric-config.get, just a different retrieval lens.
permission.act is largely planned: per-spawn mode override is built and live-verified, but allow/deny/ask rule lists and mid-session set-mode are unbuilt.
Verification rows carry states like probe-verified, live-verified, and unverified with dates and run names.
The file includes native permission semantics: deny beats bypass, allow does not constrain bypassPermissions, subagent inheritance is dangerous, plan is the safe default.
It defines two resource-level errors: permission.unsupported-mode and permission.not-exposed.
standouts: The file corrects its own representation table: the per-spawn permission_mode override is built and live-verified, while the table still says per-session override is NOT BUILT.
permission.get is not a dedicated endpoint; it is a named lens on the existing GET /health response field .permission.
permission.act is mostly a placeholder: only mode-at-spawn is built, with rule lists and mid-session changes honestly marked planned/unverified.
Strong safety framing throughout: default plan mode, deny precedence, and warnings about subagent inheritance under permissive modes.
conventions: Uses contract-entry front matter with type, resource, summary, schemes, status, and relates-to.
Wraps schemas, operations, examples, and errors in contract:schema / contract:op / contract:example / contract:error blocks.
Includes a verification table per op with state, run, date, and explanatory note.
Uses wiki-style [[...]] links to related contract entries.
Examples are synthetic while status is building, loudly noted as not captured-live.
Self-flags stale or contradictory lines inline rather than editing them out.
Maintains neutral, caveat-heavy language and avoids claiming unverified capabilities.
concerns: The representation table contains a stale contradiction with the op verification section about whether per-session permission override is built.
permission.act carries status building but liveness none and mostly planned capabilities, which may blur the contract boundary.
Heavy overlap with fabric-config.get could make resource discovery confusing for consumers.
Verification states like probe-verified and live-verified are used without being defined in this file.
notes: This contract documents the current read-only fabric permission posture and the partially built, mostly planned per-session approval controls, with explicit honesty about what is and is not wired.
questions: What is the precise operational difference between probe-verified and live-verified in this corpus?
Where and how is COMPANY_FABRIC_PERMISSION set by an operator, and what is its current value?
How would the planned mid-session set-mode control_request be serialized onto the held-open stream-json transport?
What is the full validation grammar for allow/deny/ask rule strings beyond the brief examples?
How does the fabric enforce that no consumer can widen another session's permissions today?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches, though the active surface is smaller than the named resource implies: it is currently a read lens plus a placeholder for future setters.
apparent_themes: Safety-first defaults and explicit risk warnings about permissive modes.
Honest capability accounting: built versus planned, verified versus unverified.
Permissions as a session launch attribute rather than a standalone addressable resource.
Minimal fabric-wide control with per-session granularity deferred.
Read-before-write posture: widening requires an explicit operator or spawn decision.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to give UI builders a single, honest contract for permission controls so they do not design against unimplemented per-session rule or mid-session mode features.
novelty: The self-correcting stale-note inside the representation table and the explicit lens-of-another-op treatment, combined with detailed native-permission semantics embedded in a fabric contract.
contribution: A precise, caveat-rich contract for session permission posture that records the mode enum, rule-list semantics, native evaluation order, the current read path through /health, and an honest map of which setter capabilities are built versus planned, which a fused system would keep to avoid misrepresenting available controls.
summary_for_embedding: company/ui-contract/resources/permission.md is a contract entry that defines the tool-approval posture (mode plus allow/deny/ask rule lists) for Claude Code sessions, documents the current fabric-wide read-only posture accessible via GET /health, and specifies the partially built, mostly planned per-session permission controls.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to give consumers a single place to understand which permission levers exist today and which are still planned, so UI builders do not assume unimplemented controls are available.
